title: Susceptibilities and Patterning: A Primer on Linear Response in Bayesian Learning
abstract: These notes introduce the theory of susceptibilities as developed in [arXiv:2504.18274, arXiv:2601.12703] for interpreting neural networks. The susceptibility of an observable $φ$ to a data perturbation is defined as a derivative of a posterior expectation, which by the fluctuation--dissipation theorem equals a posterior covariance. Different choices of $φ$ yield different objects: per-sample losses give the influence matrix (the Bayesian influence function of [arXiv:2509.26544]), while component-localized observables give the structural susceptibility matrix that pairs model components with data patterns. The susceptibility matrix is (up to a factor of $nβ$) the Jacobian of the map from data distributions to structural coordinates; its pseudo-inverse provides a linearized solution to the patterning problem of [arXiv:2601.13548]: finding data perturbations that produce a desired structural change. We motivate the theory from its statistical-mechanical foundations, then give a detailed exposition of susceptibilities, their empirical estimators, and their connection to the geometry of the loss landscape.
summary: This paper introduces a theory of susceptibilities to interpret how changes in input data affect the internal state of neural networks. By linking Bayesian inference to the fluctuation-dissipation theorem, it provides a way to map data perturbations to structural changes in the model. This offers a linearized approach to the 'patterning problem' in machine learning.
main_problem: The paper aims to provide a unified framework for interpreting neural networks using the theory of susceptibilities to understand how data perturbations affect model observables.
main_result: The authors establish that the susceptibility of an observable to data perturbations is equivalent to a posterior covariance via the fluctuation-dissipation theorem, and show that the susceptibility matrix acts as a Jacobian for mapping data distributions to structural coordinates.
method: The work uses statistical-mechanical foundations, specifically applying the fluctuation-dissipation theorem and linear response theory to Bayesian inference in neural networks.
model_or_system: The system is a Bayesian neural network viewed through the lens of statistical mechanics, where model parameters are treated as degrees of freedom in a posterior distribution.
key_observables: Posterior expectation of observables (phi), per-sample losses (influence matrix), and component-localized observables (structural susceptibility matrix).
important_parameters: The inverse temperature beta (n*beta scaling) and the mapping between data distributions and structural coordinates.
main_assumptions: The framework assumes a Bayesian learning setting where the posterior distribution is well-defined and subject to small data perturbations.
paper_structure: The paper begins with a motivation from statistical-mechanical foundations, followed by a detailed exposition of susceptibility theory, empirical estimators, and finally discusses connections to the geometry of the loss landscape.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
